attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-week history of progressively reduced sensation in both hands. He works as a long-distance truck driver. His medical history includes tuberculosis diagnosed two months ago and currently on treatment, recurrent upper respiratory tract infections including pneumonia left carpal tunnel release one year ago, and a previous tibial plateau fracture. He takes isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. He is a current smoker with a 20 pack-year history. He maintains a balanced diet including regular meat and vegetables but exercises infrequently.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is symmetrically reduced sensation to vibration and proprioception across both palmar and dorsal aspects of the hands. There is mild weakness in wrist flexion bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes and coordination are intact.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of his neurological symptoms?
options: Nerve compression from a Pancoast tumour
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with bilateral sensory neuropathy while on treatment for tuberculosis. Drug side effect is the most likely cause, specifically isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy. Isoniazid interferes with pyridoxine (vitamin B6) metabolism and can cause peripheral neuropathy in up to 10% of patients. This typically presents as a symmetrical sensory neuropathy affecting the extremities. The onset within two months of starting treatment is characteristic.

Nerve compression from a Pancoast tumour typically causes unilateral symptoms in the distribution of the brachial plexus. While this patient's smoking history puts him at risk of bronchogenic carcinoma, the bilateral nature of his symptoms makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy, but typically affects the lower limbs more than upper limbs and is usually accompanied by other symptoms such as macrocytic anaemia. His regular meat intake makes this diagnosis less likely.

Diabetic neuropathy is unlikely as there is no mention of diabetes in his history, and diabetic neuropathy typically develops after 10-15 years of poorly controlled disease. It also usually begins in the feet rather than hands.

Median nerve compression (carpal tunnel syndrome) would not explain bilateral symptoms, especially as he has already had surgical release on the left side. Additionally, median nerve compression does not typically cause wrist flexion weakness.

The management would include pyridoxine supplementation while continuing isoniazid therapy. All patients on isoniazid should receive prophylactic pyridoxine to prevent this complication.
learningPoints: Isoniazid commonly causes peripheral neuropathy through pyridoxine deficiency, affecting up to 10% of patients.
Pyridoxine supplementation should be prescribed to all patients on isoniazid therapy to prevent neuropathy.
Bilateral symmetrical sensory neuropathy affecting hands suggests a systemic rather than compressive cause.
references: Stettner M, Steinberger D, Hartmann CJ, Pabst T, Konta L, Hartung HP, Kieseier BC. Isoniazid-induced polyneuropathy in a tuberculosis patient - implication for individual risk stratification with genotyping? Brain Behav. 2015 Aug;5(8):e00326. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4559012/

Steichen O, Martinez-Almoyna L, De Broucker T. Neuropathie toxique induite par l'isoniazide: pensez à la prévention [Isoniazid induced neuropathy: consider prevention]. Rev Mal Respir. 2006 Apr;23(2 Pt 1):157-60. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16788441/
